name: Pulp
bio: Pulp's catalog keeps paying because its most recognizable songs remain culturally specific, lyrically sticky, and easy to revisit.
notable_for: Pulp remains closely associated with Common People and Disco 2000, which still anchor attention around the catalog.
career_highlight: Songs like Common People and Disco 2000 still help define the catalog's long-tail earnings profile.
revenue_drivers: Repeat streaming and playlist familiarity help the biggest songs keep earning after release.
Broad recognition supports social reuse, rediscovery, and steady catalog listening.
Licensing and event-driven playback can create recurring spikes.

earnings.assumptions: Estimate keeps Pulp's current headline range as the artist-side figure and models gross catalog, label, publishing, and writer lanes from that conservative annual range.

ownership.master_owner: Likely split across label, distributor, and artist-affiliated rights depending on recording era
ownership.publishing_owner: Writer and publisher splits materially affect final artist-side income
ownership.catalog_sale_status: No full catalog sale assumption baked into this modeled range
ownership.notes: Pulp's page should be read as modeled artist-side annual income, not a public royalty statement. Ownership and label terms can materially change take-home economics.
